title: Peak summer heat can increase kidney injury risk, don't ignore these early symptoms - Moneycontrol.com
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Rajasthan
full_text:
Many people don’t realise that summer heat indirectly affects kidney health. Stepping out in the sun can make you sweaty, thirsty and dehydrated. With rising temperatures, the number of patients reporting with kidney-related issues are on the rise.
Moneycontrol reached out to Dr Sheel Bhadra Jain, Senior Consultant, Nephrology & Renal Transplant, CK Birla Hospitals, Jaipur, who explained how dehydration and heat stress can affect the kidneys.
“When the body loses excess fluids through sweating and intake is not adequate, blood flow to the kidneys can be reduced. This puts added strain on kidney function and, in some cases, may even trigger acute kidney injury, especially in vulnerable individuals such as the elderly, diabetics, or those with pre-existing kidney conditions,” he said.

When you sweat excessively and do not take enough water to replace the lost fluids, you become dehydrated. “This leads to more concentrated urine, which increases the risk of kidney stone formation and urinary infections,” Dr Jain added.
Additionally, he warned that people tend to consume more sugary or caffeinated beverages during summer, which can further burden the kidneys if taken in excess.
Early warning signs are often subtle but should not be ignored. According to Dr Jain, these include:
Reduced urine output
Swelling in the feet
Persistent fatigue
Muscle cramps
Burning sensation while urinating
In some cases, people may also experience dizziness or a drop in blood pressure due to fluid imbalance.

Preventive steps are simple yet crucial. According to Dr Jain, here are some steps to take to ensure your kidneys are safe
Maintaining adequate hydration is the most important—water intake should be consistent throughout the day, not just when one feels thirsty.
Including hydrating foods like fruits and avoiding excessive salt, processed foods, and sugary drinks can also help.
People who work outdoors or exercise in the heat should take extra precautions and replenish fluids regularly.
Ultimately, kidney health in summer depends on awareness and timely action. Ignoring early signs or delaying hydration can lead to complications that are otherwise preventable with simple lifestyle adjustments.
1. How does summer heat affect kidney health?
Heat causes sweating and fluid loss. If you don’t drink enough, blood flow to the kidneys falls, straining them and potentially triggering acute kidney injury, especially in the elderly, diabetics, or those with existing kidney problems.
2. Why does dehydration increase kidney stone risk?
Dehydration makes urine more concentrated. This concentration promotes kidney stone formation and raises the risk of urinary infections, especially when fluid loss from sweating is not replaced adequately.
3. What early warning signs of kidney problems should I watch for in summer?
Watch for reduced urine output, dark-coloured urine, swelling in the feet, persistent fatigue, muscle cramps, burning while urinating, dizziness, or low blood pressure. These may signal fluid imbalance or kidney strain.
4. Do sugary or caffeinated drinks in summer harm the kidneys?
Taken in excess, sugary or caffeinated drinks can burden the kidneys, especially when they replace plain water. They should not be the main way you rehydrate in hot weather.
5. How can I protect my kidneys during hot weather?
Drink water consistently through the day, not only when thirsty. Eat hydrating fruits, avoid excessive salt, processed foods and sugary drinks, and replenish fluids regularly if you work outdoors or exercise in the heat.
